column110: Ethically, I believe offering access to all babies and families is right. It would be unethical if access was restricted. Morally, families would still have the choice to opt-in or opt-out of the screening, so they can decide what is best for them. For families who want more information about their baby, screening even with no troubling behavior is morally right. It would be morally wrong to force all babies to be screened, especially using some of the more invasive methods shown.

column117: While I may be stressed, worried, and confused, and my relationship with my baby may be different, those feelings would have still happened later down the road when the more traditional diagnostics take place. It is better for me to know now and be able to adapt and prepare for the future. The good outweighs the bad when it gives me more information to connect to resources, community, and my family sooner.
column119: Depending on what type of test was performed (blood test, brain imaging vs. doctor observation), I would have questions on how confident to be in a "change" of results. I think I would regret getting tested if it was a doctor's observation and now my child looks to be a neurotypical kindergartener. I would call that a bad (invalid) test that probably wasted a lot of time and energy for our family. I would feel less confident of a "change" if it was a blood/brain test. I would have more trust in the original results and likely question the new observations. I'd likely wonder if my child is adjusting well and appears neurotypical now, but has not yet been exposed to triggers that would cause them to exhibit signs of autism (stress, puberty, sports/groups, etc). In some ways, I would then be waiting for the signs to appear and that may cause me to regret the early screening test. Either way, a "wrong" test is a risk with anything and there is no way to know at the time of opting-in to the screening. This would be part of my informed consent to participate in the testing and a choice that I (with my family) would make.

column136: ~20%, having multiple autistic kids is not uncommon
column139: Reassurance,Awareness,Knowledge,Reduced anxiety and worry about my baby's development,Hope about baby's future

column142: The reverse side of the coin of "Reassurance" is "Relief". While I would feel less anxious and more confident in supporting my baby's future, I cannot say I wouldn't also feel relieved. It's hard not to! This could unintentionally, though, lead to a negative outlook on autism and disabilities ("thank goodness it's not us") and therefore estrangement from the autism community.
column144: This would again depend on the type of screening test that was done and the level of severity of signs that my child is exhibiting. Either way, though, thinking my child is not autistic during their early years only to find out later that they are autistic is no different from how the current system works.

column151: When it comes to conversations about autism, it's so important to always lead with sympathy, love, and understanding. For us parents, our kids truly are our world.  And when autism comes into the picture, it can sometimes feel very heavy.    Many of us carry guilt and blame ourselves, even though science is clear: autism is not caused by something parents did or didn't do. Still, those feelings are real. That's why the way professionals, friends, and even family talk about it matters - it should be done in a way that uplifts, not weighs down.    Because at the end of the day, what every parent needs most is support, compassion, and the reminder that they're not alone.

column117: I think my infant being diagnosed so early would be helpful because it would prompt me research and learn more about my child. Knowing that my child will behave and learn differently than others is information that will allow me to change my perspective and give my neurodivergent child the support they need to succeed in the future. I will probably lose time because I will need to re-evaluate how I believe "raising a child" is supposed to look like and I am sure changing my perspective and teaching myself and my autistic child will be stressful but not much more stressful than being a parent in general. column110: There are many nuances here... if the screen is effective in helping identify children with higher risk for an ASD diagnosis and this leads to better f/u and earlier family support and therapy interventions then that would likely out weigh the risks of the negative emotions a parents may experience through this process. Offering the free screenings with appropriate consent is very ethical. There are also many children who may not have any obvious signs of ASD at 4 mos and develop these characteristics at older ages so lack of delays does not matter. If the child already has delays it is likely that they need more evaluation already than a standard infant screening.

column117: Knowing as much as possible about your child and what they need to grow and succeed is the primary goal of any parent. A formal diagnosis while hard can provide resources, knowledge, and support for success that would otherwise go unexplored or underutilized. It also helps lessen the impacts of constant comparison to others, may reduce parental guilt, and allow for engagement in a new community where a child or family can be better understood. Stressors will certainly come with an ASD diagnosis but they are not less when it happens later after months or years of struggle and lack of understanding.
column119: If we had the diagnosis, had lots of therapies and support and my child no longer met criteria for diagnosis at school age then that is the best possible outcome. I would not regret that.   If  I got the diagnosis, struggled to engage with therapies because of push back from partner, lack of family support, lack of available resources etc and then found out that maybe the test had been wrong all along I would regret having the test in the first place. I would hesitate to implement or consent to a screening for an asymptomatic child if there were not adequate resources and access available to investigate further and provide helpful interventions.  As this relates to the earlier questions this would not serve any helpful purpose and would not be ethical.

column91: I think the possibility of my child having autism would make me a little nervous. I was not particularly anxious or depressed postpartum, but I could imagine how this could be a little bit of a trigger for some moms. More than that, I would have reservations about the accuracy of autism screening at that very early age. While I think it would be possible to flag children who would have more severe cases, I would be concerned about the possibility of "ruling out" children who may still be autistic. It seems like, when accurate, it would set children up on the best path to get EI services early, but on the flip side it could lead to a delay for kids whose parents thought autism was ruled out. I'd be more comfortable if my pediatrician talked to me about the studies supporting the screening and the limitations on the front end.

column110: I have slight reservations about this, as the accuracy and the detail the test provides would heavily influence how ethical and moral it is. Some people with autism require little to no intervention, and others would require extensive additional care. Performing a simple "yes or no" test for the presence of autism would not provide much value to the individual child. And of course, as the accuracy declines, the test becomes less worthwhile. On a macro level, I think there could be an inherent value for obtaining the data, but I don't necessarily believe a child should be subjected to tests that could permanently "mark" them as autistic without a medical indication supporting this. At 4 months, it would be hard for me to see why it would be a valuable use of resources. Certainly by 1 year, when children have had more time to develop and parents have a better view of their child's development in terms of milestones, there would be more evidence to support these screens.
